- Read SD-024 §"Stage 4 — Lint enforcement" and TD-024 §"Lint rule (Stage 4)"
  to confirm the closed-list of forbidden field names, the allowed
  exemptions (NoReview, PollInterval, SessionLogDirOverride), the
  legacy *Options types to flag (ExecuteBeadLoopOptions, RunOptions,
  ExecuteBeadOptions, CompareOptions, QuorumOptions), and the scope
  (cli/internal/agent/ only for *Runtime field rule).
- Inspected the existing FEAT-022 evidencelint analyzer at
  cli/tools/lint/evidencelint/ to confirm the pattern this bead asks
  the new analyzer to mirror (analysis.Analyzer + singlechecker cmd
  + analysistest fixtures under testdata/src/).
- Inspected the current state of cli/internal/agent/ to enumerate the
  *Runtime structs (ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime, AgentRunRuntime,
  ExecuteBeadRuntime, CompareRuntime, QuorumRuntime) and the legacy
  *Options types still present.

(b) What is blocking
The bead's final acceptance item — "Running runtimelint against the
post-cleanup tree exits 0" — cannot be satisfied because the
post-cleanup tree it presumes does not yet exist:

  Stage 2 (ddx-ade5ebb3, "agent: retire RunOptions + CompareOptions +
  QuorumOptions + legacy Run") is marked closed in the bead tracker
  but the retirement was never actually committed. The recorded
  Commit b199cf3c is a tracker-update commit that touches only
  .ddx/beads.jsonl; no code change retiring the types is anywhere in
  the git history (verified via git log --all --grep).

  Concretely, in the current worktree base (e9f2d53f):
    cli/internal/agent/types.go:36   type RunOptions struct
    cli/internal/agent/types.go:81   type QuorumOptions struct
    cli/internal/agent/types.go:171  type CompareOptions struct
  And dozens of composite-literal usages of those types across cli/cmd,
  cli/internal/agent, cli/internal/server, cli/internal/exec, and the
  agent test files. Per the bead description, the runtimelint analyzer
  is required to flag *exactly these patterns* (rule 2: composite
  literal of any legacy *Options type; rule 3: function signature
  parameter of any legacy *Options type). Running it against the
  current tree would produce dozens of legitimate diagnostics, so the
  AC "exits 0" is unachievable as long as Stage 2 remains undone.

  Stage 3 (ddx-a8eaef0a, ExecuteBeadOptions retirement) appears done
  (no struct definition for ExecuteBeadOptions in the tree) — but the
  bead's status fields show execute-loop-last-status: execution_failed
  and a BLOCK review finding flagging missing build/test evidence, so
  its closure may also be premature. ExecuteBeadLoopOptions (Stage 1,
  ddx-edbfa046) does appear truly retired.

The blocker is therefore solely Stage 2 (ddx-ade5ebb3): re-run that
bead so RunOptions/CompareOptions/QuorumOptions and their callsites
are actually removed before runtimelint enforcement is added on top.

Committing the analyzer now without that prerequisite would either:
  (i) produce a CI-red Lefthook hook that blocks every commit on the
      legacy-options usage that has not yet been migrated, or
 (ii) require gutting the rules the bead description explicitly calls
      for (rules 2 and 3), reducing the analyzer to rule 1 only and
      silently dropping the regression-prevention guarantee that is
      the entire point of Stage 4.

Per the execute-bead instructions, "a well-justified no_changes is
better than a bad commit" — neither option above meets the bead
contract honestly, so no commit is made.

   remaining callsites in cli/cmd, cli/internal/server,
   cli/internal/exec, and test files. Verify Stage 2 acceptance:
     rg 'type (RunOptions|CompareOptions|QuorumOptions) ' cli/  → 0
     cd cli && go build ./... && go test ./...                  → green

2. Verify Stage 3 (ddx-a8eaef0a) is in fact green — re-run the build
   and test ACs to clear the BLOCK finding on its evidence bundle.

   - cli/tools/lint/runtimelint/analyzer.go  — closed-list field

     in cli/internal/agent/, plus composite-literal and func-param
     checks for the five legacy *Options names. The closed list of
     forbidden field names is fixed by TD-024 §Lint rule:
       Harness, Model, Provider, ModelRef, Profile, Effort,
       Permissions, Timeout, WallClock, ContextBudget, MinTier,
       MaxTier, Assignee, ReviewMaxRetries, NoProgressCooldown,
       MaxNoChangesBeforeClose, HeartbeatInterval, SessionLogDir,
       MirrorCfg, Models, ReasoningLevels, Endpoints,
       ProfileLadders, ModelOverrides, PerHarness.
     Exempt names: NoReview, PollInterval, SessionLogDirOverride.
   - cli/tools/lint/runtimelint/analyzer_test.go  — analysistest
     fixtures under testdata/src/ exercising each rule (positive +
     negative).
   - cli/tools/lint/runtimelint/cmd/runtimelint/main.go  —
     singlechecker entry point matching evidencelint's pattern.
   - lefthook.yml  — add runtimelint hook alongside the existing
     evidencelint pre-commit hook.
   - CI workflow (.github/workflows/*.yml)  — add a runtimelint job
     mirroring the evidencelint job.
   - Final verification:
       cd cli && go build ./tools/lint/runtimelint/...        → ok
       cd cli && go test  ./tools/lint/runtimelint/...        → ok
       cd cli && go run ./tools/lint/runtimelint/cmd/runtimelint ./...

.ddx/executions/20260426T182313-e23c9642/result.json:9:  "no_changes_rationale": "Bead: ddx-b8926c2d — agent: final sweep — runtimelint passes against the tree
Decision: no_changes (blocked on prerequisite work)

## What is done
- Ran `go run ./tools/lint/runtimelint/cmd/runtimelint ./...` against the post-cleanup tree.
- Confirmed the bead's AC (`exits 0`) is NOT met: the lint reports 88 violations and exits with status 3.

## What is blocking
The bead's notes explicitly warn that B22 closed as already_satisfied without actually retiring the legacy options types. The current tree still uses `agent.RunOptions` extensively:

- Composite-literal usages (RunOptions{...}) across production code:
  cli/internal/agent/{compare_adapter,execute_bead,execute_bead_review,grade,runner,service_run}.go
  cli/internal/exec/store.go
  cli/internal/server/server.go
  cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go
- Function parameters typed as the legacy options across ~15 production files
  (agent_runner.go, agent_runner_service.go, claude_stream.go, compare_adapter.go,
   execute_bead.go, execute_bead_review.go, models.go, routing_metrics.go,
   runner.go, script.go, service_run.go, session_index.go, virtual.go, ...)
- Same pattern repeated across ~20 test files (agent_test.go, claude_stream_test.go,
  models_test.go, prompt_ingress_oversize_test.go, session_index_test.go,
  virtual_test.go, execute_bead_*_test.go, etc.)

Total: 88 violations. This is exactly the state the bead's "REOPENED 2026-04-26" note
predicts: "runtimelint actually run produces ~80 violations against the tree because
B22 didn't actually retire RunOptions/CompareOptions/QuorumOptions."

## Why this is not a final-sweep task
The "final sweep" bead is intentionally narrow: rerun the lint and assert green to
catch drift introduced after the migration is done. It is not the bead that performs
the migration. Resolving 88 violations across ~25 production files plus ~20 test files
requires:
  - Threading *Runtime + ResolvedConfig through every harness, service, runner,
    compare adapter, execute_bead, claude_stream, virtual, session_index, models,
    routing_metrics, script, exec/store, server, and cmd/agent_cmd call site.
  - Updating every test fixture that constructs a RunOptions{...}.
  - Removing the legacy types themselves once all references are gone.
That is the body of B22, not a sweep. Doing it inside this bead would silently absorb
the entirety of a separate migration bead and mask the fact that B22 is incomplete —
which is precisely the problem the reopened note flags.

## What a follow-up attempt would need
1. B22 (or a successor) must actually land: migrate all RunOptions/CompareOptions/
   QuorumOptions call sites to *Runtime + config.ResolvedConfig and delete the
   legacy types. The runtimelint output above is an exhaustive checklist of the
   remaining call sites.
2. Once B22 is genuinely green (lint exits 0 on the tree), this final-sweep bead
   becomes a one-liner: re-run the same command and confirm no new drift appeared.

Re-queueing this bead now would just produce another no_changes (or, worse, an
attempt that absorbs B22's scope under the wrong bead id). Recommend keeping it
blocked on B22 until B22 ships for real.",
.ddx/executions/20260426T184511-edb52c8c/manifest.json:71:          "body": "Bead: ddx-ade5ebb3 — agent: retire RunOptions + CompareOptions + QuorumOptions + legacy Run

NO CHANGES — bead preconditions not satisfied; scope exceeds single-window budget.

## What is done

- Read the bead, SD-024, TD-024, runtimelint analyzer, and current state of
  cli/internal/agent/types.go and runner.go.
- Surveyed actual caller surface in the worktree (HEAD = 562f293c).

## Why no commit was made

The bead description states explicit dependencies:

  "Depends on bead 16 (Stage 1 cleanup) AND beads 17-21 (every Stage 2
   caller migrated)."

The bead's own reopen note (2026-04-26) confirms those dependencies are
NOT satisfied:

  "type RunOptions still exists at cli/internal/agent/types.go:36;
   QuorumOptions at :81; CompareOptions at :171. runtimelint reports
   ~80 violations across the tree using these types. Closure was fake
   (already_satisfied or premature). The retirement was NOT actually
   performed — only ExecuteBeadLoopOptions (B16) and ExecuteBeadOptions
   (B28) shipped real type-deletion commits."

So B17-B21 (the per-caller Stage 2 migration beads) were never done.
Today's measured surface, from this worktree:

  rg "RunOptions|CompareOptions|QuorumOptions" cli/  →  188 references
                                                        across 48 files
  rg "RunOptions\{|CompareOptions\{|QuorumOptions\{" cli/
                                                     →  ~50 composite literals
                                                        (~13 production,
                                                         ~31 in tests,
                                                         ~5 in lint testdata,
                                                         rest in agent
                                                         package internals)

Production constructor sites:
  cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1462
  cli/internal/agent/compare_adapter.go:61, 261, 414, 685
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:647
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:531
  cli/internal/agent/grade.go:169
  cli/internal/agent/service_run.go:82
  cli/internal/exec/store.go:201
  cli/internal/server/server.go:1913, 3786

Internal-implementation surface (beyond the two files the bead names as
"in-scope"): every dispatch helper in the agent package threads
RunOptions through its signature —

  agent_runner.go, agent_runner_service.go, claude_stream.go,
  compare_adapter.go, execute_bead.go, routing_metrics.go, runner.go,
  script.go, service_run.go, session_index.go, virtual.go

plus BuildArgs, RunAgent, runVirtualFn, runScriptFn,
runClaudeWithFallbackFn, finalizeClaudeResult, processResult, and
TestProviderConnectivity all take RunOptions or fields from it.
CompareOptions and QuorumOptions both EMBED RunOptions, so deleting
RunOptions also forces a redesign of compare_adapter.go's option flow.

## What is blocking

The bead is framed as a one-shot type-deletion ("delete the structs,
rename RunWithConfig → Run"), but the AC requires `go build` and
`go test` to pass tree-wide. To pass those gates the bead has to
also:

1. Replace every internal RunOptions parameter (agent package
   helpers) with an AgentRunRuntime + ResolvedConfig pair, OR keep
   them on a non-RunOptions internal type.
2. Migrate all ~13 production caller sites to RunWithConfig
   (renamed Run), including building a config.ResolvedConfig at each
   site (CLI, server, exec store, grade, compare_adapter,
   execute_bead, execute_bead_review, service_run).
3. Migrate or delete the CompareOptions/QuorumOptions surface
   (compare_adapter.go is heavily built around it; this is essentially
   a rewrite of the comparison/quorum dispatcher, not a rename).
4. Migrate ~31 test composite literals across agent_test.go,
   claude_stream_test.go, prompt_ingress_oversize_test.go,
   session_index_test.go, models_test.go, virtual_test.go,
   plus runtime tests.
5. Update or remove evidencelint analyzer entries for RunOptions
   and the runtimelint testdata (which deliberately uses the legacy
   types as fixtures).

This is the SD-024 §"Migrate one production caller per bead" / §"Migrate
test files in groups" work — the ~5 beads (B17–B21) the bead description
points at as completed dependencies. Doing all of that here in a single
execute-bead window violates the SD's own per-bead size budget
("<500 LoC of net change, <20 minutes of agent time, under $10 of model
spend") by an order of magnitude. The realistic LoC delta is in the
1500–2500 range across 30+ files.

## What a follow-up attempt would need

Reopen B17–B21 (or their successor beads) with concrete per-file scopes
before re-queuing this bead. Suggested split:

- B17: migrate cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go RunViaService caller +
       cli/internal/server/server.go (2 sites). Add a
       config.ResolvedConfig handoff at each site.
- B18: migrate cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go +
       execute_bead_review.go + service_run.go (3 sites; agent-package
       internal callers).
- B19: migrate cli/internal/agent/compare_adapter.go and
       cli/internal/agent/grade.go — biggest surface; redesigns
       CompareOptions/QuorumOptions on top of AgentRunRuntime
       (likely promotes Harnesses/Strategy/Threshold/ArmModels/
       ArmLabels/Sandbox/KeepSandbox/PostRun onto a new
       CompareRuntime/QuorumRuntime).
- B20: migrate cli/internal/exec/store.go + types.go.
- B21: migrate the ~31 test composite literals; update
       evidencelint analyzer + testdata.

Once those land green, this bead (B22) becomes the trivially-correct
final cleanup — delete the three types, rename RunWithConfig → Run,
update the runtimelint testdata that intentionally uses the legacy
names, and confirm runtimelint reports zero violations on the live
tree.

Recommendation: do NOT escalate this bead to a stronger model yet.
The blocker is the missing prior beads, not model capability. A
stronger model attempting the full ~2000-LoC migration in one
worker window would produce churn that is hard to review and likely
breaks one of the three production dispatch sites in a non-obvious
way (the historical bug class SD-024 was created to prevent).

.ddx/executions/20260426T110750-b9397620/result.json:9:  "no_changes_rationale": "## What is done

The bead's implementation work is already merged on the base branch. Commit
`63e86ba1 refactor(agent): retire ExecuteBeadOptions and legacy ExecuteBead
[ddx-a8eaef0a]` removed both:

  - `type ExecuteBeadOptions` struct
  - the legacy `ExecuteBead` (non-`WithConfig`) method

Verification of AC item 1:

    $ rg 'type ExecuteBeadOptions ' cli/
    (no matches)

The remaining `ExecuteBeadOptions` mentions in the tree are only in source
comments (cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_e2e_test.go, .../execute_bead_e2e_routing_test.go,
cli/internal/config/types.go, cli/internal/config/testconfig.go) referencing
the SD-024 migration history — the type itself is gone.

## What is blocking

AC items 2 and 3 (`go build ./...` / `go test ./...`) cannot be demonstrated
green in this pass for a reason unrelated to this bead's scope:

    cli/cmd/agent_route_status.go:326:28: cand.RecentSuccessRate undefined
        (type "github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent".RouteCandidateStatus has
         no field or method RecentSuccessRate)
    cli/cmd/agent_route_status.go:421:12: cand.RecentSuccessRate undefined

The same failure is present at the bead's declared base-rev
(`7bb075002d99bf66d84a860b4364c430ba637fa9`), so it predates the
ExecuteBeadOptions removal and is not caused by this work.

Root cause: the external `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` module renamed
`RouteCandidateStatus.RecentSuccessRate` → `ProviderReliabilityRate` (per
ADR-006 §5, in `/Users/erik/Projects/agent/service.go`). The local
`cli/cmd/agent_route_status.go` still references the old field name. This is
a separate routing-observability migration, outside the SD-024 cleanup scope
named by this bead's description ("Removes the legacy ExecuteBeadOptions
struct and the legacy ExecuteBead method").

Spot check that the affected packages alone are healthy:

    $ go build ./internal/agent/... ./internal/config/...
    (exit 0)

    $ go test ./internal/agent/ -run TestSynthesizeCommitMessage_InExecuteBead -count=1
    ok  	github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/internal/agent	0.011s

So the SD-024-touched code is green; only the unrelated `cmd/agent_route_status.go`
breaks the whole-tree build/test commands the AC names.

## What a follow-up attempt would need

Either:

  (a) File and complete a separate bead that updates
      `cli/cmd/agent_route_status.go` to use
      `RouteCandidateStatus.ProviderReliabilityRate` (two call sites:
      lines 326 and 421), then re-queue this bead so its full-tree
      build/test commands can be demonstrated green; or

  (b) Relax this bead's AC to scope-limited build/test commands
      (`go build ./internal/agent/...` and
      `go test ./internal/agent/...`), since the legacy-code removal
      itself does not touch the broken package.

Committing a route-status fix from inside this bead would violate the
"Do not modify files outside the scope the bead description names"
constraint in the execute-bead instructions.",
.ddx/executions/20260426T184511-edb52c8c/result.json:9:  "no_changes_rationale": "no_changes rationale — ddx-ade5ebb3 (Stage 2 retire RunOptions/CompareOptions/QuorumOptions + legacy Run)

WHAT IS DONE
- Surveyed full call graph for the three doomed types and the legacy Run method.
- Confirmed bead notes: dependencies (beads 17–21) ARE all closed, but they migrated
  only the public CLI command paths (`ddx agent run`, `ddx agent compare`,
  `ddx agent quorum`) — not the internal agent-package plumbing nor the
  external server/exec callers.

WHAT IS BLOCKING
The bead's stated in-scope is two files (cli/internal/agent/types.go,
cli/internal/agent/runner.go), but the AC requires `cd cli && go build ./...`
and `cd cli && go test ./...` to pass after the three exported types and the
legacy Run method are removed. Reality of references that must move
together (ripgrep counts):

A. agent.RunOptions / CompareOptions / QuorumOptions — 88 references in
   non-testdata Go files outside the lint testdata trees. Of these:

   Internal to cli/internal/agent/ (cannot be deleted without parallel rewrite):
   - virtual.go (runVirtualFn signature + builder)
   - script.go (runScriptFn signature)
   - claude_stream.go (4 helpers: resolveClaudeProgressLogDir,
     runClaudeStreamingFn, runClaudeWithFallbackFn, finalizeClaudeResult)
   - agent_runner.go (RunAgent), agent_runner_service.go (runAgentViaService)
   - models.go (BenchmarkArmsToCompare → CompareOptions)
   - grade.go (r.Run(RunOptions{...}))
   - service_run.go (RunViaService, RunViaServiceWith, runFixtureHarnessViaRunner — public exported entry points)
   - session_index.go (SessionIndexEntryFromResult — exported)
   - routing_metrics.go (recordRoutingOutcome)
   - compare_adapter.go (~25 references — RunFunc, RunCompareWith, RunCompareViaService, RunCompareWithAgent, defaultResolvePromptForCompare, runCompareArmWith, RunQuorumWith, RunQuorumViaService, RunQuorumWithAgent, RunBenchmarkWith, plus the existing RunCompareWithConfigViaService/RunQuorumWithConfig adapters that still build CompareOptions/QuorumOptions internally)
   - execute_bead.go (AgentRunner interface signature + dispatchAgentRun + the runOpts builder around line 647)
   - execute_bead_review.go (DefaultBeadReviewer.dispatchReviewRun + the AgentRunner field at line 425)
   - runner.go itself (resolveHarness, resolvePrompt, resolveModel, resolveTimeout, resolveWallClock, BuildArgs, recordRoutingOutcome, TestProviderConnectivity, plus the legacy Run body and the Run alias for AgentRunner satisfaction)

   External callers (cli/internal/server, cli/internal/exec, cli/cmd):
   - cli/internal/server/server.go:1913 + :3786 (handleAgentRun + workers handler — both build agent.RunOptions{} for RunViaService)
   - cli/internal/exec/types.go:11 (AgentRunner interface signature uses agent.RunOptions)
   - cli/internal/exec/store.go:201 (builds agent.RunOptions for s.AgentRunner.Run)
   - cli/cmd/exec.go:98 (serviceExecAgentRunner.Run wraps RunViaService)
   - cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1462 (RunViaService call building RunOptions)

   Test mocks that implement AgentRunner (must update their Run signature
   in lockstep with the interface change):
   - cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_runtime_test.go (capturingAgentRunner)
   - cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review_test.go (reviewRunnerStub)
   - cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review_evidence_test.go (countingRunner)
   - cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_env_isolation_test.go (envIsoRunner)
   - cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_e2e_test.go (gateTestAgentRunner, rationaleTestRunner)
   - cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_artifacts_test.go (artifactTestAgentRunner)
   - cli/internal/agent/executions_mirror_test.go (artifactTestAgentRunner usage)
   - cli/internal/exec/store_test.go (mockAgentRunner)
   - cli/cmd/exec_acceptance_test.go (mockAgentRunnerCmd)
   - cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go (fakeAgentRunner, modelPassthroughCapture — also captures `last agent.RunOptions`)
   - cli/internal/agent/agent_test.go (run-closure for RunCompareWith on line 584)
   - Plus the BuildArgs(h, RunOptions{...}, "") test fixtures across agent_test.go (~16 sites) — these use the struct literal form, not interface satisfaction, so they migrate trivially via field-by-field replacement, but they still count.

B. Legacy Runner.Run satisfies agent.AgentRunner interface today. Several
   tests pass *Runner directly into ExecuteBeadRuntime{AgentRunner: runner}
   (integration_helper_test.go:184, execute_bead_runtime_test.go:56,
   execute_bead_routing_evidence_test.go:35/85/116,
   execute_bead_e2e_test.go:358/403). Removing the legacy Run AND renaming
   RunWithConfig → Run breaks this satisfaction unless either:
     (a) The agent.AgentRunner interface itself is migrated to take
         (ctx, ResolvedConfig, AgentRunRuntime) — cascades to every mock
         listed in (A) above, AND to cli/internal/exec/types.go's
         AgentRunner because cmd/agent_execute_bead.go:152 assigns one to
         the other (Go's structural interface satisfaction).
     (b) An adapter type is introduced (e.g., agent.RunnerAsAgentRunner)
         and every test wiring rewritten — ~10 test sites.

WHY THIS IS A NO_CHANGES, NOT A BAD COMMIT
Two prior attempts on this bead were "fake-closed" (per the bead notes) —
likely because they tried to satisfy the AC verifier via the loophole that
the field_removed regex `\bX\s+[\*\[\]\w.]+` does not match
`type X = Y` aliases or `(opts X)` parameter lists. A type-alias
relocation (defining the structs in cli/internal/agentopts/ and aliasing
them back into the agent package) WOULD satisfy the regex check and the
manual rg over types.go alone, while keeping every external caller
working. But that is the same loophole that the bead author explicitly
called out and reopened twice for. The bead notes say: "Worker must
actually delete the three type definitions and migrate every call site."
Doing that requires the multi-file refactor described above.

That refactor is well-defined but is at least 30 files, ~150 hunks of
edits, and depends on a coordinated public-API redesign of:
  - agent.RunViaService / RunViaServiceWith (signature change)
  - agent.AgentRunner interface (signature change, cascades to exec.AgentRunner)
  - cli/internal/exec.AgentRunner interface (signature change)
  - The Runner.Run / Runner.RunWithConfig swap with a new private
    runInternal(opts) for internal callers (grade.go, service_run.go,
    runner.go itself, execute_bead_review.go) so they don't build a fake
    ResolvedConfig just to dispatch.

That is genuinely epic-sized — bigger than the four Stage-2 dependency
beads put together — and committing it half-done in this single worktree
risks leaving the build red on main.

WHAT A FOLLOW-UP ATTEMPT WOULD NEED
Either:

(1) Re-decompose this bead into ≥5 smaller beads that can each land green:
    - 24a "agent: introduce private runInternal + new agent.AgentRunner signature with RunArgs adapter type"
    - 24b "exec: migrate AgentRunner interface to (ctx, rcfg, runtime) signature; update store + cmd/exec wrapper"
    - 24c "server: migrate handleAgentRun + workers handler to agent.RunViaServiceWithConfig"
    - 24d "agent: migrate internal helpers (virtual, script, claude_stream, agent_runner, models, grade, service_run, session_index, routing_metrics, compare_adapter) off RunOptions/CompareOptions/QuorumOptions struct names"
    - 24e (this bead) "agent: delete the three exported type definitions + delete legacy Runner.Run + rename RunWithConfig → Run"
    Each prerequisite bead has a clear gate-verifiable AC and the final
    bead becomes a 2-file mechanical removal as originally scoped.

(2) Or: explicitly authorize this bead to expand its scope to the full
    30-file refactor and accept that it will land as one large PR. The
    bead description's "In-scope" lines and the AgentRunner interface

.ddx/executions/20260426T185836-ae76e2f1/manifest.json:13:    "description": "**Reporter**: nexiq (downstream consumer). Observed across 2026-04-22 → 2026-04-26 on master.

## Observed

`ddx agent execute-bead` can land a commit locally (worktree merged into local master), then fail at the `git push origin master` step, and still classify the bead as `execute-bead: success`. The push failure shows up as `landed locally; push failed:` in the event body, but the bead transitions to closed and the loop moves on.

Concrete blast radius: ~969 unpushed commits accumulated on nexiq's master across multiple days because every bead that landed in that window had a 258 MB turbopack `.sst` file somewhere in its commit history. GitHub rejected each push with the 100 MB-per-file limit error. The execute-loop's success rate looked normal — operators saw bead = closed, dashboard = working — but origin was untouched.

Recovery required `git filter-branch --index-filter 'git rm --cached -r packages/web/.next .next'` across ~1000 commits plus a `.gitignore` hardening change. By that point the only signal that something was wrong was an operator manually running `git rev-list --count origin/master..master`.

## Root cause

The execute-bead orchestrator currently classifies the result of the *merge* step. The push step happens after, and a non-zero exit there is logged in the event body but does not bubble up to the bead's terminal status. As far as routing/state machines are concerned, the bead is done.

This is the worst-case classification: silent for operators, irreversible for state (bead closes, primary doesn't re-run, the work that needs to be re-pushed isn't tracked anywhere).

## Failure modes this masks

- Large blob in commit history (the case we hit) — every subsequent bead inherits the bad ancestor and fails to push.
- Auth expiration / credential rotation on the origin remote.
- Branch protection rules added on the remote between the loop's last successful push and now.
- HTTP 5xx / network flake against origin.
- Local divergence from origin (someone else pushed; non-fast-forward refused).

In all five cases the current behavior is: bead closes, loop picks the next bead, the divergence grows monotonically.

## Suggested fix

1. Promote push to a first-class step in execute-bead's outcome classification.
   - On non-zero exit from `git push`, emit `execute-bead: push_failed` (or similar terminal that explicitly is NOT success) with the push stderr captured in the event body.
   - Reopen the bead with `last_status=push_failed` and a retry_after that buys time for an operator to investigate.
2. Surface push failure on the next claim attempt: when a worker tries to claim a bead in `last_status=push_failed`, refuse the claim and show the operator the prior push stderr — don't blindly redo the primary work, since the local commit is already there.
3. (Optional, defense in depth) Have execute-loop maintain a per-repo divergence counter (`git rev-list --count origin/<branch>..<branch>`); if it crosses a threshold (e.g. >5), pause new claims and emit a loop-level warning event.

## Reproduction

1. On a local fork, install a `pre-receive` hook on the remote that rejects all pushes (or use a remote that requires a permission the runner doesn't have).
2. Queue any bead and run execute-loop normally.
3. Observe: bead closes as success despite the push failing. The merge commit lives only on local master.
4. Confirm: `git rev-list --count origin/master..master` is non-zero, but ddx still routed a new worker to the next bead.

## Files to inspect

- nexiq commit `f2c2ed33` (the .gitignore + .next scrub) is the recovery commit on the downstream side.
- nexiq's own follow-up bead (`nexiq-e57b41eb`) lists this as a blocker; I'll amend it once this ddx bead has an ID.

## Acceptance

- A forced push failure (e.g. pre-receive hook rejecting) results in a bead that does NOT show as closed and shows up in `ddx bead list --status open` with a `last_status=push_failed` field.
- The operator can see the push stderr in the bead's events.
- Subsequent claim attempts on that bead fail loudly until the operator clears the state (retry, force-skip, etc.).",

.ddx/executions/20260429T162702-2c963978/manifest.json:14:    "description": "execute-loop's adaptive min-tier promotion (escalation.AdaptiveMinTierThreshold etc.) silently skips cheap tier when trailing cheap-tier success rate falls under threshold. Today the only visible signal is a single log line:

  adaptive min-tier: skipping cheap tier (trailing success rate 0.00 over 9 attempts; threshold 0.20) — min-tier=standard

There is no documented or discoverable way to reset this state. Issues with the current design:

1. STATE LOCATION IS NOT DOCUMENTED. The trailing-window metric is computed from .ddx/agent-logs/routing-outcomes.jsonl per-project (and possibly other sources — rotating that file alone did NOT clear the 0% verdict in a recent test, so additional state may live elsewhere). A user who wants to reset cannot find authoritative documentation of where the state lives.

2. NO RESET CLI. There is no `ddx agent route-status reset`, no `ddx agent metrics clear`, no `ddx agent doctor --reset-adaptive`. The only path is to edit/truncate JSONL files by hand, which is fragile and breaks compatibility with any future schema.

3. SILENT NOISE. Every cheap-tier failure permanently affects future routing without surfacing to the user. If a configuration bug (e.g. the model_overrides issue tracked as ddx-5538aa5b) caused 9 immediate failures, the adaptive logic now blocks cheap tier even after the configuration is fixed. The user has no way to know 'this is stale state, not real performance signal' without reading source.

4. MITIGATION VIA --no-adaptive-min-tier IS NOT A FIX, IT'S AN OVERRIDE. The flag bypasses the gating but also defeats the legitimate purpose of the metric. We need ability to RESET the metric and re-evaluate from a clean slate, not permanently disable the feature.

5. THE THRESHOLD/WINDOW DEFAULTS ARE AGGRESSIVE. Trailing window of 9-50 attempts at threshold 0.20 means a single cheap-tier provider misconfiguration (which is easy to hit, see ddx-5538aa5b) condemns cheap-tier for many subsequent invocations. Meanwhile EVERY configuration error is recorded as cheap-tier failure even when the failure was 'no viable provider' rather than 'model produced bad output'.

Requested behavior:

- A first-class CLI: `ddx agent route-status reset [--scope cheap|all]` that clears the adaptive metric for the current project, with a confirm prompt by default.
- A flag on `ddx agent doctor` that displays current adaptive state per tier (success rate, attempt count, threshold, current floor) so users can SEE the problem.
- An option to record only EXECUTION outcomes (model produced output) in adaptive metrics, NOT no-viable-provider/configuration failures — those should be tracked separately and never count against a tier's reputation.
- Documented location and schema for the metric store, so users who want to inspect or surgically edit understand the on-disk format.",
.ddx/executions/20260429T162702-2c963978/manifest.json:15:    "acceptance": "AC1. `ddx agent route-status reset` (or equivalent verb) exists and clears the trailing-window adaptive metric for the current project, returning min-tier evaluation to a clean baseline. Output names every file/store touched.

AC2. `ddx agent doctor` (or `ddx agent route-status`) prints current adaptive state per tier: window size, trailing attempts, success rate, threshold, current effective floor, and whether the tier is currently being skipped. Diagnostic output is enough to debug a 'why is cheap being skipped' scenario without reading source.

AC3. Routing failures classified as 'no viable provider', 'harness not installed', or 'configuration error' are recorded but EXCLUDED from the adaptive success-rate computation — only failures that reached an actual model invocation count.

AC4. Documentation in docs/ describes the adaptive min-tier mechanism, the metric store path and schema, the reset workflow, and the relationship between --no-adaptive-min-tier (temporary bypass) and reset (permanent state clear).

AC5. After running the reset CLI, a fresh `execute-loop --once --local` evaluates cheap tier on its own merits without the prior trailing-window verdict carrying over.",

in-scope ("cli/tools/lint/runtimelint/", "lefthook.yml — add hook",
"CI workflow — add job"). Doing it under this bead would silently
expand scope and risk a poor merge.

Why no CI block entry was added either
--------------------------------------

A `runtimelint-all` entry in lefthook.yml's `ci:` block would mirror
`evidence-lint-all` (lefthook.yml:425-430) and is the cleanest CI
wiring (the GitHub Actions workflow already runs `lefthook run ci`,
so no .github/workflows/ci.yml change is needed once the lefthook ci
block is populated). However, adding it now would put CI red against
the same 30+ legitimate diagnostics described above. AC4 explicitly
requires "Running runtimelint against the post-cleanup tree exits 0";
that is the gate that licenses turning on the CI block.

Wiring the CI block before the retirement beads land would also
silently break ddx-development unrelated to SD-024 (any PR rebased
on main would fail the new gate), violating the staged-rollout
principle that the staged-file-scoped pre-commit hook already
respects.

Recommended path forward
------------------------

This bead's analyzer + pre-commit hook should stand as-is in main
(they already do, via ec83a30b6fc81dddfc8415599dfcbff8a7f36978).
What is needed is one or two new tracker beads:

  1. Retire RunOptions: replace remaining test-site composite
     literals with the *Runtime + ResolvedConfig pair; remove the
     internal use inside the *Runtime adapters; delete the type
     declaration. Acceptance: `go run ./tools/lint/runtimelint/
     cmd/runtimelint ./...` produces no RunOptions diagnostics.

  2. Retire CompareOptions and QuorumOptions: same pattern; delete
     the `opts.RunOptions = RunOptions{...}` style internal
     composition in cli/internal/agent/compare_adapter.go and
     quorum_adapter.go; remove the type declarations.
     Acceptance: `runtimelint ./...` exits 0.

  3. Then a tiny follow-up bead enables the CI gate by adding a
     `runtime-lint-all` entry under `ci:` in lefthook.yml mirroring
     `evidence-lint-all`.

Reopening this bead a second time without doing that retirement
work first will not change the answer. The analyzer is built; the
hook is wired; the missing piece is RunOptions/CompareOptions/
QuorumOptions retirement, which is explicitly listed as a
dependency on this bead and is not part of its in-scope.

  compute failure_classes counts keyed on context_overflow,
  provider_empty, unparseable, transport.

- A new CLI command (FEAT-022 §17 explicitly says "no new command").

- GraphQL exposure of the aggregated metrics — separate work if
  needed.

.ddx/executions/20260425T100025-2a058341/manifest.json:13:    "description": "During ddx agent execute-bead and ddx agent execute-loop, write a .ddx/run-state.json file at execution start and remove it on completion. This file records the currently-executing bead ID, attempt ID, harness, start time, and execution worktree path so operators and HELIX can observe what is currently running without polling the bead tracker.

This capability is implied by CONTRACT-001 Section 5 (Always-on runtime metrics and provenance) but is currently absent from the DDx source. The post-execution evidence bundle (.ddx/executions/) captures historical facts, but there is no live-state signal during execution.

## Scope

- On execute-bead start: write .ddx/run-state.json with fields: bead_id, attempt_id, harness, model, started_at (RFC3339), worktree_path
- On execute-bead end (success or failure): remove .ddx/run-state.json (or overwrite with status=idle)
- execute-loop may write N run-state entries or a single active one depending on parallelism model
- File must be written atomically (write tmp + rename) to avoid partial-read races

## Contract reference

CONTRACT-001 Section 5: Always-on runtime metrics and provenance — per-attempt capture of harness, model, session ID, elapsed, tokens, cost, base revision, result revision.",

## Scope

- On execute-bead start: write .ddx/run-state.json with fields: bead_id, attempt_id, harness, model, started_at (RFC3339), worktree_path
- On execute-bead end (success or failure): remove .ddx/run-state.json (or overwrite with status=idle)
- execute-loop may write N run-state entries or a single active one depending on parallelism model

## Contract reference

CONTRACT-001 Section 5: Always-on runtime metrics and provenance — per-attempt capture of harness, model, session ID, elapsed, tokens, cost, base revision, result revision.
.ddx/executions/20260425T073541-4f46941d/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User directives on fixture size and storage strategy

Two standing instructions from the user that apply to this bead and to all other perf beads (`ddx-ad0db8fd`, `ddx-2ceb02fa`, and any spawned from efficacy/providers work):

1. **Minimum fixture size: 1,000s of records.** The original acceptance criterion (10 projects × 500 beads = 5,000) already meets this for beads, but restate the floor explicitly: **no perf work lands against a fixture smaller than 1,000 records per query shape**. Sessions fixtures, efficacy fixtures, provider/quota history fixtures — all ≥1k rows. Beads fixture stays at 5k; sessions fixture for `ddx-2ceb02fa` should be ≥10k index entries (already specified); efficacy and any new surfaces ≥1k attempts/rows each.

2. **DB backend is on the table.** If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, lightweight index) cannot hit the target latencies on the fixture sizes above, the user is open to introducing a real data store — candidates named: **axon**, **sqlite**, **postgres**. Do not silently pick one: **raise the decision with the user before choosing a path.** Capture the baseline, capture what in-place optimization achieved, then bring the choice (and the rationale) to the user.

These directives supersede any acceptance criterion that implies "optimize in place no matter what." If the numbers say storage needs to change, that's the outcome — but the user decides the substrate.

**AC §8 — Playwright cross-project test routes wrong path**: `beads-smoke.spec.ts:107` navigates to `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/projects/${PROJECT_ID}/beads` for the "cross-project" test, which is the same per-project URL as the 1s test. A cross-project smoke test should hit the all-beads route (e.g., `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/beads` or whatever the actual cross-project URL is) and mock the `beads` query, not `beadsByProject`. The mock for `beads` exists (lines 76-89) but is never exercised because both tests navigate to the project-scoped path.
**AC §9 — Bead notes cross-reference**: The acceptance criteria require this bead's notes to list `ddx-ad0db8fd` and `ddx-2ceb02fa`. The baseline `.md` and code comments mention them, but no `ddx bead update` call or notes update appears in the diff. If the notes were updated via a separate mechanism not captured in this branch, this finding can be dismissed.

## 2026-04-23 — Baseline and perf-thread cross-reference

Baseline recorded in `docs/helix/06-iterate/perf/2026-04-22-baseline.md` and JSON sibling from the 10 projects x 500 beads fixture (5,000 beads total, 1,000 session rows). Environment: `go=go1.26.2 goos=linux goarch=arm64 cpu-cores=12`.

Key p95 results: `bead` 6.72ms in-process / 6.80ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 8.84ms in-process / 9.29ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 62.16ms in-process / 75.80ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.45ms in-process / 6.87ms HTTP.

Cross-reference for inherited harness work: `ddx-ad0db8fd` covers single-bead detail lookup; `ddx-2ceb02fa` covers sessions shard/index work. Both should use/extend this GraphQL perf harness.

Review follow-up: AC §8 cross-project Playwright smoke now navigates to `/nodes/{nodeId}/beads` and exercises the `beads` GraphQL query instead of the project-scoped `beadsByProject` path. Proposed CI posture remains a follow-up +25% p95 regression gate, not implemented in this bead.

## 2026-04-23 — Baseline refresh after session-index fixture alignment

Re-ran `BENCH_REPORT_DATE=2026-04-22 make bench-graphql` after updating the perf fixture to seed current pointer-only session-index shards instead of legacy `sessions.jsonl`. Refreshed artifacts: `docs/helix/06-iterate/perf/2026-04-22-baseline.md` and `.json`.

Refreshed p95 results: `bead` 7.23ms in-process / 7.29ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 7.18ms in-process / 7.83ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 62.49ms in-process / 67.29ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.74ms in-process / 7.73ms HTTP; `agentSessions` 13.45ms in-process / 14.19ms HTTP.

## 2026-04-23 — Final `make bench-graphql` verification

After fixing `cli/Makefile` to pass an absolute `DDX_BENCH_REPORT_DIR`, `BENCH_REPORT_DATE=2026-04-22 make bench-graphql` writes to the intended repo docs path. Final p95 results from that run: `bead` 6.76ms in-process / 7.20ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 6.88ms in-process / 7.85ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 63.22ms in-process / 71.82ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.75ms in-process / 7.56ms HTTP; `agentSessions` 13.24ms in-process / 13.64ms HTTP.

REVIEW:BLOCK

Review payload is insufficient: the only commit shown is the execution-evidence chore. Re-run the review against the full branch diff (commits `9dc0a408` and any predecessors introducing the harness, `BeadsByProject` scope fix, filter pushdown, `make bench-graphql`, baseline markdown, and Playwright specs) so each AC can be graded against real code.
Execution events in `manifest.json` also record a prior iter-1 finding (Playwright cross-project test in `beads-smoke.spec.ts:107` navigated to the per-project URL rather than the all-beads route) and a notes-cross-reference gap; confirm both are resolved in the full diff before approving AC §8 and §9.

.ddx/executions/20260423T030704-e84fa494/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../workers` renders a table of workers but has no buttons, no controls, and no explanation of how it differs from `/sessions`. The layout (`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte`) shows ID / Kind / State / Current Bead / Attempts — read-only. Clicking a row drills into live-streaming logs and recent events, but offers no actions there either.

This raised a fair question: **what is this screen for, and how is it different from Sessions?** The answer isn't in the UI.

## Ground truth (from the CLI)

- **Worker** = a persistent execute-loop process draining the bead queue (`ddx work` / `ddx agent execute-loop`). Has a lifecycle: running / idle / stopped / error. Has a *current bead* and cumulative attempts/successes/failures. Can be stopped via `ddx agent workers stop`. Long-lived.
- **Session** = a historical record of one agent invocation (harness, model, bead, tokens, cost, outcome, start/end). Immutable after completion. One worker produces many sessions over its lifetime.

So: Workers are *processes you operate*. Sessions are *events you review*. The current UI exposes both as flat tables and lets the reader guess which is which.

## Gaps

1. **No disambiguation.** No page-level copy, no link between the two, no worker→sessions navigation ("show sessions produced by this worker"), no session→worker backlink.
2. **No lifecycle controls on Workers.** The CLI has `ddx agent workers stop` and `ddx work` (start). The UI has neither button. No stop, start, restart, or "cancel current bead" affordance. The State column is a spectator sport.
3. **No worker start UX.** There is no way to start a queue drain from the UI — users must drop to a terminal.
4. **Unclear empty state.** "No workers found." does not say whether that is normal (nothing running) or broken (server can't see workers). No hint about how to start one.
5. **No worker details beyond logs.** Worker detail page shows live phase + log tail + tool calls but not: worker config (project path, profile/harness, started-at, last-heartbeat), resource usage, or the list of sessions this worker has produced.

## Proposed direction

Split into two deliverables — do them together if we're touching this screen anyway, but they are independently landable:

### A. Information architecture (copy + navigation)

- Add a short header description to each page: "Workers drain the bead queue. Sessions are the history of what they ran."
- Mutual links: Workers page links to "Recent sessions →", Sessions page links to "Workers →".
- On worker detail: a "Sessions" tab or section listing sessions where `session.workerId == this.id` (requires session records to carry workerID; check whether they do).
- On session detail (expandable row already exists): if the session has a workerID, surface it as a link back to the worker.

### B. Lifecycle controls

- **Stop** button per running worker row → calls a GraphQL mutation that invokes the equivalent of `ddx agent workers stop <id>`. Confirms before acting.
- **Start worker** button in the page header → opens a small form (project, profile/harness, effort, optional bead filter) and calls a `startWorker` mutation that shells out to `ddx work` in the background. The form should default to sane values and be submittable in one click for the common case.
- **Cancel current bead** action on the worker detail page — signals the worker to abandon the in-flight bead without killing the loop. (Only if CLI primitive exists; otherwise descope and note in the bead.)
- All mutations must be idempotent and log an audit record viewable in the UI (who did it, when). Reuse existing session-event plumbing if possible.

### Out of scope

- Rewriting execute-loop semantics.
- Cross-project worker orchestration.
- Auth / permissions model for who can stop workers (assume single-operator for now; file a follow-up if multi-operator becomes real).",

.ddx/executions/20260423T021809-c3ed3f02/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../workers` renders a table of workers but has no buttons, no controls, and no explanation of how it differs from `/sessions`. The layout (`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte`) shows ID / Kind / State / Current Bead / Attempts — read-only. Clicking a row drills into live-streaming logs and recent events, but offers no actions there either.

This raised a fair question: **what is this screen for, and how is it different from Sessions?** The answer isn't in the UI.

## Ground truth (from the CLI)

- **Worker** = a persistent execute-loop process draining the bead queue (`ddx work` / `ddx agent execute-loop`). Has a lifecycle: running / idle / stopped / error. Has a *current bead* and cumulative attempts/successes/failures. Can be stopped via `ddx agent workers stop`. Long-lived.
- **Session** = a historical record of one agent invocation (harness, model, bead, tokens, cost, outcome, start/end). Immutable after completion. One worker produces many sessions over its lifetime.

So: Workers are *processes you operate*. Sessions are *events you review*. The current UI exposes both as flat tables and lets the reader guess which is which.

## Gaps

1. **No disambiguation.** No page-level copy, no link between the two, no worker→sessions navigation ("show sessions produced by this worker"), no session→worker backlink.
2. **No lifecycle controls on Workers.** The CLI has `ddx agent workers stop` and `ddx work` (start). The UI has neither button. No stop, start, restart, or "cancel current bead" affordance. The State column is a spectator sport.
3. **No worker start UX.** There is no way to start a queue drain from the UI — users must drop to a terminal.
4. **Unclear empty state.** "No workers found." does not say whether that is normal (nothing running) or broken (server can't see workers). No hint about how to start one.
5. **No worker details beyond logs.** Worker detail page shows live phase + log tail + tool calls but not: worker config (project path, profile/harness, started-at, last-heartbeat), resource usage, or the list of sessions this worker has produced.

## Proposed direction

Split into two deliverables — do them together if we're touching this screen anyway, but they are independently landable:

### A. Information architecture (copy + navigation)

- Add a short header description to each page: "Workers drain the bead queue. Sessions are the history of what they ran."
- Mutual links: Workers page links to "Recent sessions →", Sessions page links to "Workers →".
- On worker detail: a "Sessions" tab or section listing sessions where `session.workerId == this.id` (requires session records to carry workerID; check whether they do).
- On session detail (expandable row already exists): if the session has a workerID, surface it as a link back to the worker.

### B. Lifecycle controls

- **Stop** button per running worker row → calls a GraphQL mutation that invokes the equivalent of `ddx agent workers stop <id>`. Confirms before acting.
- **Start worker** button in the page header → opens a small form (project, profile/harness, effort, optional bead filter) and calls a `startWorker` mutation that shells out to `ddx work` in the background. The form should default to sane values and be submittable in one click for the common case.
- **Cancel current bead** action on the worker detail page — signals the worker to abandon the in-flight bead without killing the loop. (Only if CLI primitive exists; otherwise descope and note in the bead.)
- All mutations must be idempotent and log an audit record viewable in the UI (who did it, when). Reuse existing session-event plumbing if possible.

### Out of scope

- Rewriting execute-loop semantics.
- Cross-project worker orchestration.
- Auth / permissions model for who can stop workers (assume single-operator for now; file a follow-up if multi-operator becomes real).",

.ddx/executions/20260423T035053-7b39eb3f/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User-directed amendment: shard the index

Per user feedback: a single `sessions.index.jsonl` just replays the same problem at smaller scale. The aggregate needs to be split.

### Revised storage shape

- Directory: `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/` (per-project, under each registered project's `.ddx`).
- Inside: **monthly shards** named `sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`.
- Each shard is pointer-only, same fields as the previously-proposed index (id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath, nativeLogRef).
- Writer resolves the target shard from `time.Now().UTC()` at append time and creates the shard file lazily on first write of the month.
- Heavy bodies (prompt, response, stderr) stay where they already live — `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/` and agent-log streams — and are lazy-loaded by the `session(id:)` resolver.

### Why monthly, not daily / size-based / SQLite

- **Daily** — produces tens of thousands of files over the project lifetime; ReadDir becomes the cost.
- **Size-based** — requires coordinating rotation with the append; race-prone and hard to test.
- **SQLite** — overkill for first pass; revisit if a single monthly shard grows past tens of MB.
- **Monthly** — ≤12 shards/year, human-readable filenames, date-range queries map trivially to shard selection, bounded shard size in practice.

### Reader behavior

- Default Sessions query loads the **current + previous shard** (covers "recent activity" for any current date).
- Date-range queries map the range to a shard set and read only those.
- Pagination beyond the default window triggers older-shard loads on demand.
- No shard-coordination lock needed: each month's file is append-only and only the current-month shard is actively written.

### Legacy migration

- `ddx agent log reindex` reads the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` once and splits by each entry's `timestamp` field into the appropriate monthly shards under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/`.
- After migration, the legacy file is renamed `sessions.jsonl.legacy` (not deleted) and never read again by the server. Hand-removal is the user's call.
- Idempotent: re-running the command merges without duplicating (index row dedup by `id`).

### Revised acceptance criteria (supersedes criteria 1, 5, 6, 7 on original description)

1. **Sharded index writer.** Single shared call site (from `service.Execute` or equivalent wrapper) appends one line to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, computing the shard from `time.Now().UTC()`. Test: write across a simulated month boundary and assert two shards exist with the correct split.

5. **Legacy migration shards too.** `ddx agent log reindex` splits `sessions.jsonl` into monthly shards by entry timestamp. Integration test uses a fixture with entries across 3 distinct months and asserts 3 shards are produced with correct contents and no duplicates on re-run.

6. **Server reads the shard directory.** `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` reads `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/*.jsonl`, respects any date-range arg, and loads only the shards that intersect the range. Unit test seeds 6 monthly shards and asserts a 2-month query reads exactly 2 files.

7. **Perf on sharded index.** Default Sessions view (current + previous shard) resolves in ≤50ms p95 on a fixture with 12 monthly shards × 10k entries each (120k total). Capture baseline (single-file read of same corpus) vs sharded in bead notes.

(Criteria 2, 3, 4, 8, 9 from the original acceptance list remain as written.)

### Scope clarifier

This amendment does not change the single-call-site writer guarantee or the lazy-body resolver — those remain the load-bearing ideas. The amendment is specifically about **how the pointer index is physically stored**.
## 2026-04-23 — Implementation benchmark and evidence

Baseline: `go test ./internal/agent -run '^$' -bench 'Benchmark(ReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow|LegacySessionsJSONLFullScan)$' -benchtime=1x` measured legacy single-file full scan/sort of 120k legacy-shaped rows with 2KB embedded responses at `BenchmarkLegacySessionsJSONLFullScan-12 1 1731705957 ns/op`.

After fix: the same command measured the sharded default-window reader at `BenchmarkReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow-12 1 8103661 ns/op` for 12 monthly shards x 10k rows each (120k total), with the default query reading only the current + previous shard window. Environment footer: linux/arm64, Go go1.26.2.

Acceptance evidence: `TestSessionIndexWritesMonthlyShards`, `TestSessionIndexShardFilesSelectsOnlyIntersectingDateRange`, `TestReindexLegacySessionsSplitsShardsAndDedupes`, `TestRunViaServiceWithAppendsOneSessionIndexRow`, `TestProductionAgentExecutionPathsUseIndexedServiceWriter`, `TestGraphQLAgentSessions`, `TestAgentLogReindexMigratesLegacyFile`, existing agent usage/status/processmetrics tests, and `e2e/sessions.spec.ts` cover writer sharding, bounded reads, migration, lazy body loading, deprecated-file read removal, UI gap banner, and live refresh.

REVIEW:BLOCK

Missing session index writer: no changes to `service.Execute`, shared execution wrappers, or agent execution paths to append `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`.
Missing GraphQL changes: no update to `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go` for index-backed `sessions` or lazy `session(id:)` body loading.
Missing migration command: no `ddx agent log reindex` or equivalent CLI implementation and no fixture-based migration test.
Missing CLI usage/status updates: no changes to `cli/cmd/agent_usage.go` or `cli/cmd/status.go` to stop reading legacy `sessions.jsonl`.
Missing performance validation: no benchmark or fixture proving 10,000 index entries resolve within the required threshold.
Missing UI gap/banner behavior and Playwright coverage for `/sessions`, lazy loading, migration, and live feed updates.

.ddx/executions/20260423T035055-06c99b23/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User directives on fixture size and storage strategy

Two standing instructions from the user that apply to this bead and to all other perf beads (`ddx-ad0db8fd`, `ddx-2ceb02fa`, and any spawned from efficacy/providers work):

1. **Minimum fixture size: 1,000s of records.** The original acceptance criterion (10 projects × 500 beads = 5,000) already meets this for beads, but restate the floor explicitly: **no perf work lands against a fixture smaller than 1,000 records per query shape**. Sessions fixtures, efficacy fixtures, provider/quota history fixtures — all ≥1k rows. Beads fixture stays at 5k; sessions fixture for `ddx-2ceb02fa` should be ≥10k index entries (already specified); efficacy and any new surfaces ≥1k attempts/rows each.

2. **DB backend is on the table.** If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, lightweight index) cannot hit the target latencies on the fixture sizes above, the user is open to introducing a real data store — candidates named: **axon**, **sqlite**, **postgres**. Do not silently pick one: **raise the decision with the user before choosing a path.** Capture the baseline, capture what in-place optimization achieved, then bring the choice (and the rationale) to the user.

These directives supersede any acceptance criterion that implies "optimize in place no matter what." If the numbers say storage needs to change, that's the outcome — but the user decides the substrate.

**AC §8 — Playwright cross-project test routes wrong path**: `beads-smoke.spec.ts:107` navigates to `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/projects/${PROJECT_ID}/beads` for the "cross-project" test, which is the same per-project URL as the 1s test. A cross-project smoke test should hit the all-beads route (e.g., `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/beads` or whatever the actual cross-project URL is) and mock the `beads` query, not `beadsByProject`. The mock for `beads` exists (lines 76-89) but is never exercised because both tests navigate to the project-scoped path.
**AC §9 — Bead notes cross-reference**: The acceptance criteria require this bead's notes to list `ddx-ad0db8fd` and `ddx-2ceb02fa`. The baseline `.md` and code comments mention them, but no `ddx bead update` call or notes update appears in the diff. If the notes were updated via a separate mechanism not captured in this branch, this finding can be dismissed.

would unwind the amendment that the bead owner explicitly requested and would
reintroduce the single-file aggregate they asked us to split. That is not a
correct change. A no_changes attempt with the evidence above is the right
signal here.

Suggested follow-up
-------------------
If the next review still fires BLOCK, the review prompt should be grounded
against the amended acceptance criteria (notes section, 2026-04-22 amendment
— supersedes original AC 1/5/6/7). The amendment documents exactly why the
index is sharded under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/` instead of a flat
`sessions.index.jsonl`.",
.ddx/executions/20260423T040710-68db6897/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User-directed amendment: shard the index

Per user feedback: a single `sessions.index.jsonl` just replays the same problem at smaller scale. The aggregate needs to be split.

### Revised storage shape

- Directory: `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/` (per-project, under each registered project's `.ddx`).
- Inside: **monthly shards** named `sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`.
- Each shard is pointer-only, same fields as the previously-proposed index (id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath, nativeLogRef).
- Writer resolves the target shard from `time.Now().UTC()` at append time and creates the shard file lazily on first write of the month.
- Heavy bodies (prompt, response, stderr) stay where they already live — `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/` and agent-log streams — and are lazy-loaded by the `session(id:)` resolver.

### Why monthly, not daily / size-based / SQLite

- **Daily** — produces tens of thousands of files over the project lifetime; ReadDir becomes the cost.
- **Size-based** — requires coordinating rotation with the append; race-prone and hard to test.
- **SQLite** — overkill for first pass; revisit if a single monthly shard grows past tens of MB.
- **Monthly** — ≤12 shards/year, human-readable filenames, date-range queries map trivially to shard selection, bounded shard size in practice.

### Reader behavior

- Default Sessions query loads the **current + previous shard** (covers "recent activity" for any current date).
- Date-range queries map the range to a shard set and read only those.
- Pagination beyond the default window triggers older-shard loads on demand.
- No shard-coordination lock needed: each month's file is append-only and only the current-month shard is actively written.

### Legacy migration

- `ddx agent log reindex` reads the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` once and splits by each entry's `timestamp` field into the appropriate monthly shards under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/`.
- After migration, the legacy file is renamed `sessions.jsonl.legacy` (not deleted) and never read again by the server. Hand-removal is the user's call.
- Idempotent: re-running the command merges without duplicating (index row dedup by `id`).

### Revised acceptance criteria (supersedes criteria 1, 5, 6, 7 on original description)

1. **Sharded index writer.** Single shared call site (from `service.Execute` or equivalent wrapper) appends one line to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, computing the shard from `time.Now().UTC()`. Test: write across a simulated month boundary and assert two shards exist with the correct split.

5. **Legacy migration shards too.** `ddx agent log reindex` splits `sessions.jsonl` into monthly shards by entry timestamp. Integration test uses a fixture with entries across 3 distinct months and asserts 3 shards are produced with correct contents and no duplicates on re-run.

6. **Server reads the shard directory.** `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` reads `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/*.jsonl`, respects any date-range arg, and loads only the shards that intersect the range. Unit test seeds 6 monthly shards and asserts a 2-month query reads exactly 2 files.

7. **Perf on sharded index.** Default Sessions view (current + previous shard) resolves in ≤50ms p95 on a fixture with 12 monthly shards × 10k entries each (120k total). Capture baseline (single-file read of same corpus) vs sharded in bead notes.

(Criteria 2, 3, 4, 8, 9 from the original acceptance list remain as written.)

### Scope clarifier

This amendment does not change the single-call-site writer guarantee or the lazy-body resolver — those remain the load-bearing ideas. The amendment is specifically about **how the pointer index is physically stored**.
## 2026-04-23 — Implementation benchmark and evidence

Baseline: `go test ./internal/agent -run '^$' -bench 'Benchmark(ReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow|LegacySessionsJSONLFullScan)$' -benchtime=1x` measured legacy single-file full scan/sort of 120k legacy-shaped rows with 2KB embedded responses at `BenchmarkLegacySessionsJSONLFullScan-12 1 1731705957 ns/op`.

After fix: the same command measured the sharded default-window reader at `BenchmarkReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow-12 1 8103661 ns/op` for 12 monthly shards x 10k rows each (120k total), with the default query reading only the current + previous shard window. Environment footer: linux/arm64, Go go1.26.2.

Acceptance evidence: `TestSessionIndexWritesMonthlyShards`, `TestSessionIndexShardFilesSelectsOnlyIntersectingDateRange`, `TestReindexLegacySessionsSplitsShardsAndDedupes`, `TestRunViaServiceWithAppendsOneSessionIndexRow`, `TestProductionAgentExecutionPathsUseIndexedServiceWriter`, `TestGraphQLAgentSessions`, `TestAgentLogReindexMigratesLegacyFile`, existing agent usage/status/processmetrics tests, and `e2e/sessions.spec.ts` cover writer sharding, bounded reads, migration, lazy body loading, deprecated-file read removal, UI gap banner, and live refresh.

.ddx/executions/20260424T032813-9a7b8808/manifest.json:13:    "description": "During ddx agent execute-bead and ddx agent execute-loop, write a .ddx/run-state.json file at execution start and remove it on completion. This file records the currently-executing bead ID, attempt ID, harness, start time, and execution worktree path so operators and HELIX can observe what is currently running without polling the bead tracker.

This capability is implied by CONTRACT-001 Section 5 (Always-on runtime metrics and provenance) but is currently absent from the DDx source. The post-execution evidence bundle (.ddx/executions/) captures historical facts, but there is no live-state signal during execution.

## Scope

- On execute-bead start: write .ddx/run-state.json with fields: bead_id, attempt_id, harness, model, started_at (RFC3339), worktree_path
- On execute-bead end (success or failure): remove .ddx/run-state.json (or overwrite with status=idle)
- execute-loop may write N run-state entries or a single active one depending on parallelism model
- File must be written atomically (write tmp + rename) to avoid partial-read races

## Contract reference

CONTRACT-001 Section 5: Always-on runtime metrics and provenance — per-attempt capture of harness, model, session ID, elapsed, tokens, cost, base revision, result revision.",

.ddx/executions/20260423T005714-24085f34/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User directives on fixture size and storage strategy

Two standing instructions from the user that apply to this bead and to all other perf beads (`ddx-ad0db8fd`, `ddx-2ceb02fa`, and any spawned from efficacy/providers work):

1. **Minimum fixture size: 1,000s of records.** The original acceptance criterion (10 projects × 500 beads = 5,000) already meets this for beads, but restate the floor explicitly: **no perf work lands against a fixture smaller than 1,000 records per query shape**. Sessions fixtures, efficacy fixtures, provider/quota history fixtures — all ≥1k rows. Beads fixture stays at 5k; sessions fixture for `ddx-2ceb02fa` should be ≥10k index entries (already specified); efficacy and any new surfaces ≥1k attempts/rows each.

2. **DB backend is on the table.** If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, lightweight index) cannot hit the target latencies on the fixture sizes above, the user is open to introducing a real data store — candidates named: **axon**, **sqlite**, **postgres**. Do not silently pick one: **raise the decision with the user before choosing a path.** Capture the baseline, capture what in-place optimization achieved, then bring the choice (and the rationale) to the user.

These directives supersede any acceptance criterion that implies "optimize in place no matter what." If the numbers say storage needs to change, that's the outcome — but the user decides the substrate.

**AC §8 — Playwright cross-project test routes wrong path**: `beads-smoke.spec.ts:107` navigates to `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/projects/${PROJECT_ID}/beads` for the "cross-project" test, which is the same per-project URL as the 1s test. A cross-project smoke test should hit the all-beads route (e.g., `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/beads` or whatever the actual cross-project URL is) and mock the `beads` query, not `beadsByProject`. The mock for `beads` exists (lines 76-89) but is never exercised because both tests navigate to the project-scoped path.
**AC §9 — Bead notes cross-reference**: The acceptance criteria require this bead's notes to list `ddx-ad0db8fd` and `ddx-2ceb02fa`. The baseline `.md` and code comments mention them, but no `ddx bead update` call or notes update appears in the diff. If the notes were updated via a separate mechanism not captured in this branch, this finding can be dismissed.

.ddx/executions/20260422T210022-1dd87663/manifest.json:14:    "description": "## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../workers` renders a table of workers but has no buttons, no controls, and no explanation of how it differs from `/sessions`. The layout (`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte`) shows ID / Kind / State / Current Bead / Attempts — read-only. Clicking a row drills into live-streaming logs and recent events, but offers no actions there either.

This raised a fair question: **what is this screen for, and how is it different from Sessions?** The answer isn't in the UI.

## Ground truth (from the CLI)

- **Worker** = a persistent execute-loop process draining the bead queue (`ddx work` / `ddx agent execute-loop`). Has a lifecycle: running / idle / stopped / error. Has a *current bead* and cumulative attempts/successes/failures. Can be stopped via `ddx agent workers stop`. Long-lived.
- **Session** = a historical record of one agent invocation (harness, model, bead, tokens, cost, outcome, start/end). Immutable after completion. One worker produces many sessions over its lifetime.

So: Workers are *processes you operate*. Sessions are *events you review*. The current UI exposes both as flat tables and lets the reader guess which is which.

## Gaps

1. **No disambiguation.** No page-level copy, no link between the two, no worker→sessions navigation ("show sessions produced by this worker"), no session→worker backlink.
2. **No lifecycle controls on Workers.** The CLI has `ddx agent workers stop` and `ddx work` (start). The UI has neither button. No stop, start, restart, or "cancel current bead" affordance. The State column is a spectator sport.
3. **No worker start UX.** There is no way to start a queue drain from the UI — users must drop to a terminal.
4. **Unclear empty state.** "No workers found." does not say whether that is normal (nothing running) or broken (server can't see workers). No hint about how to start one.
5. **No worker details beyond logs.** Worker detail page shows live phase + log tail + tool calls but not: worker config (project path, profile/harness, started-at, last-heartbeat), resource usage, or the list of sessions this worker has produced.

## Proposed direction

Split into two deliverables — do them together if we're touching this screen anyway, but they are independently landable:

### A. Information architecture (copy + navigation)

- Add a short header description to each page: "Workers drain the bead queue. Sessions are the history of what they ran."
- Mutual links: Workers page links to "Recent sessions →", Sessions page links to "Workers →".
- On worker detail: a "Sessions" tab or section listing sessions where `session.workerId == this.id` (requires session records to carry workerID; check whether they do).
- On session detail (expandable row already exists): if the session has a workerID, surface it as a link back to the worker.

### B. Lifecycle controls

- **Stop** button per running worker row → calls a GraphQL mutation that invokes the equivalent of `ddx agent workers stop <id>`. Confirms before acting.
- **Start worker** button in the page header → opens a small form (project, profile/harness, effort, optional bead filter) and calls a `startWorker` mutation that shells out to `ddx work` in the background. The form should default to sane values and be submittable in one click for the common case.
- **Cancel current bead** action on the worker detail page — signals the worker to abandon the in-flight bead without killing the loop. (Only if CLI primitive exists; otherwise descope and note in the bead.)
- All mutations must be idempotent and log an audit record viewable in the UI (who did it, when). Reuse existing session-event plumbing if possible.

### Out of scope

- Rewriting execute-loop semantics.
- Cross-project worker orchestration.
- Auth / permissions model for who can stop workers (assume single-operator for now; file a follow-up if multi-operator becomes real).",

Two standing instructions from the user that apply to this bead and to all other perf beads (`ddx-ad0db8fd`, `ddx-2ceb02fa`, and any spawned from efficacy/providers work):

These directives supersede any acceptance criterion that implies "optimize in place no matter what." If the numbers say storage needs to change, that's the outcome — but the user decides the substrate.

.ddx/executions/20260415T062435-4255f769/manifest.json:14:    "description": "The ddx-bead skill guides bead creation but underspecifies what makes a bead *execution-ready* for automated execute-bead runs. Observed during axon ADR-018 execute-loop driving: beads that technically met the current skill's requirements (title, AC, labels, description pointing at spec-id) still produced bad execute-bead outcomes because the description lacked the context the agent needed to ship correct code in one pass.

## Gaps in the current skill

1. **Description quality is underspecified.** The skill gives AC quality rules but the description field just says 'per SD-004 Section 2' is OK. In practice, agents need:
   - Exact file paths to touch (e.g., 'crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs')
   - Cross-bead context when dependencies are listed (e.g., 'A1 landed the credential_revocations table in crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs — query it via SELECT 1')
   - Inline spec excerpts, NOT just doc-id references. If the spec is ADR-018 §4's failure table, paste the table into the description. Don't say 'see ADR-018 §4'.
   - Files NOT to touch (scope negation)

2. **spec-id and the doc graph are unreliable for execution context.** The current skill implies spec-id linkage is sufficient. It is not — spec-id drives docgraph.BuildGraphWithConfig() which only resolves HELIX-shaped doc references. Generic beads on non-HELIX projects have no equivalent. AND even when resolution works, the agent only gets a file path, not the relevant section. The bead description is the only place that carries content the agent can rely on.

3. **No guidance on cross-bead context.** When bead B depends on bead A, B's description should describe what A landed (file paths, type names, key APIs) — not assume the agent will reopen A's history. Automated execute-bead does not have that context; each bead is read cold.

4. **No guidance on verification commands in AC.** AC items like 'unit tests green' are too vague. Should be 'cargo test -p axon-server green' or 'bun test passes in sdk/typescript'. Named commands.

5. **No guidance on bead size.** A bead should be one unit of testable work, sized so a single agent pass can complete it. Too small is overhead; too large defeats review. Heuristic: if the AC lists more than ~12 checkboxes, consider splitting.

6. **No guidance on incorporating review findings on reopen.** When a bead is reopened after a REQUEST_CHANGES review, the author should either (a) fold the review findings into the description for the next attempt to see, or (b) rely on the tool to thread review events into the prompt (a separate ddx bead — see ddx-a317208c). The skill is silent on this.

7. **No template for description.** A recommended description template (Scope / Files to touch / Cross-bead context / Verification / Quality bar) would make good beads cheaper to author.

## Scope

Update skills/ddx-bead/SKILL.md to add:

- A 'What makes a bead execution-ready' section that codifies the points above
- A 'Description template' with recommended sections: Scope, Files to touch, Files NOT to touch, Cross-bead context, Verification commands, Spec excerpts inline
- A 'Bead sizing' section with the ~12-AC heuristic
- A 'Review-driven reopen' section that tells authors to update the description in place when a bead is reopened after a review rejection
- A 'Common anti-patterns' section enumerating the mistakes observed during the axon execute-loop:
  - Listing doc IDs without inlining the relevant text
  - Not naming file paths in the description
  - AC items that are not verifiable by a named command
  - Depending on unmerged work without explaining what it landed

Keep the existing required-fields and label-conventions sections — they are correct.

## Context

Observed during axon ADR-018 execute-loop:
- Bead A1 was well-sized but initially had scope creep in its description (audit_retention_policies, which belonged to F1). Agent added the scope-creep table faithfully.
- Bead A2's initial description said 'UserIdentity { provider, subject, user_id }' — a typo carried over from an earlier ADR revision. Agent implemented 'subject' faithfully even though ADR-018 §3 said 'external_id'. The description was the contract the agent followed.
- Bead B1 is being re-authored right now with a richer description (inline ADR-018 §4 failure table, exact file paths, cross-bead context about A1 schema and A2 types) — this is the experiment to validate the authoring changes proposed in this bead.

## Acceptance

- [ ] skills/ddx-bead/SKILL.md gains 'What makes a bead execution-ready' section
- [ ] Description template added with the 6 recommended sections
- [ ] Bead sizing heuristic documented
- [ ] Review-driven reopen guidance added
- [ ] Common anti-patterns enumerated
- [ ] Skill still lints against any existing skill-format CI check",
